response: Given quantities are \(a_{10} = 10\), and \(a_n = 100a_{n-1} + n\) for positive integer \(n > 10\). We first need to find the most general form of \(a_n\). By iterating the recurrence, we observe: \[ a_{11} = 100a_{10} + 11 = 100 \cdot 10 + 11 = 1011, \] \[ a_{12} = 100a_{11} + 12 = 100 \cdot 1011 + 12 = 101112, \] \[ a_{13} = 100a_{12} + 13 = 100 \cdot 101112 + 13 = 10111213, \ldots \] The recurrence sequence \(a_n\) for \(n > 10\) appears to be \(a_n = 10^{n-10} \cdot a_{10} + 10^{n-11} \cdot 11 + 10^{n-12} \cdot 12 + \cdots + n\). Simplifying with \(a_{10} = 10\): \[ a_n = 10^{n-10} \cdot 10 + 10^{n-11} \cdot 11 + 10^{n-12} \cdot 12 + \cdots + n. \] We need to find the smallest positive integer \(n > 10\) such that \(a_n \equiv 0 \pmod{99}\). Given \(a_n = 10^{n-10} \cdot 10 + 10^{n-11} \cdot 11 + 10^{n-12} \cdot 12 + \cdots + n\), and since \(100 \equiv 1 \pmod{99}\), \(a_n \pmod{99}\) is just the sum of digits \(11 + 12 + \cdots + n\) modulo 99 because \(10^k \equiv 1 \pmod{99}\) for any positive integer \(k\). Hence, the condition reduces to \[ a_n \equiv 10 + 11 + 12 + \cdots + n \equiv 0 \pmod{99}. \] The sum of an arithmetic series is given by the formula \(\text{Sum} = \frac{n(n + 1)}{2}\). Therefore, we need \[ 10 + \frac{n(n + 1)}{2} \equiv 0 \pmod{99}. \] Simplifying, we get \[ \frac{n(n + 1)}{2} \equiv -10 \pmod{99} \implies n(n + 1) \equiv -20 \pmod{198} \implies n(n + 1) \equiv 178 \pmod{198}. \text{ The least positive } n > 10 \text{ solving this is } n = 73. </think> <answer> 73 </answer>
